title: CIL System — Full-length Mock 06
pattern: CIL MT — 200 MCQ · 2 papers · 1 mark each · no negative marking

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: In code, 'coal mining' = pk st, 'deep mining' = st mz, 'coal reserve' = pk nq. Code for 'reserve':
questions.solution: nq

questions.stem: An examination has 6 subjects. A student must pass at least 4. How many combinations of passing subjects?

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Statement: The stock market reached an all-time high this quarter. Conclusion I: The economy is performing well. II: All companies are profitable.
questions.solution: Neither follows

questions.stem: Find the wrong number: 5, 15, 30, 80, 160, 480

questions.stem: In code, 'equal rights' = rt eq, 'right now' = nw rt, 'now or never' = nw or nr. Code for 'equal':
questions.solution: eq

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: A manager has ₹50,000 to distribute among 3 projects. Each project needs at least ₹10,000 and amounts in multiples of ₹5,000. Number of ways:

questions.stem: Statement: A government report shows 40% of coal mine workers suffer from respiratory issues. Conclusion I: Coal mining causes health problems. II: Respiratory issues are endemic to mining areas.
questions.solution: Only I follows

questions.stem: Two positions of a dice show 1 opposite 6, 2 opposite 5, 3 opposite 4. Which number is opposite 6?

questions.stem: In a 3×3 grid of squares, how many rectangles?

questions.stem: Find the missing: 1, 2, 5, 14, 41, ?

questions.stem: In a code, DELHI = 73541 and CALCUTTA = 82589662, then CALICUT = ?

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: A company policy says employees with >5 years tenure get extra leave. Ram has 4 years 11 months experience. Is he eligible?
questions.solution: No

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Statement: Several states have announced drought relief packages. Conclusion I: These states are facing drought. II: Central government will also announce relief.
questions.solution: Only I follows

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: A 10×10×10 cube is painted and cut into 1×1×1 cubes. Cubes with at least one face painted:

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Oxygen : Combustion :: Hydrogen : ?
questions.solution: Combustion

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Should India increase coking coal imports to boost steel production? Arg I: Yes, domestic coking coal is limited. Arg II: No, it affects the trade deficit.
questions.solution: Both are strong

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Celsius : Temperature :: Richter : ?
questions.solution: Earthquake intensity

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Eight people in square arrangement: P, Q, R, S corners; T, U, V, W edges. P opposite R. Q adjacent to P. T between P and Q. U opposite T. Who is between Q and R?
questions.solution: V

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Statements: Some pens are pencils. All pencils are erasers. Conclusions: I. Some erasers are pens. II. No pencil is a pen.
questions.solution: Only I follows

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Deep: Ocean :: High : ?
questions.solution: Mountain

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Ananya faces east. She turns 180° clockwise, then 90° anticlockwise, then 45° clockwise. Now facing:
questions.solution: South-west

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Statements: Some cats are dogs. All dogs are animals. No animal is bird. Conclusions: I. Some cats are not birds. II. Some animals are cats.
questions.solution: Both follow

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Statements: All birds have wings. All animals with wings can fly. Some animals that fly are not birds. Conclusions: I. All birds can fly. II. Some flying animals are not birds.
questions.solution: Both follow

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Correct: No sooner he arrived than the meeting started.
options: No sooner did he arrived than the meeting started.
No sooner he had arrived than the meeting started.
No sooner he arrives than the meeting started.
No sooner had he arrived than the meeting started.
questions.solution: No sooner had he arrived than the meeting started.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: She is interested ____ pursuing higher studies.
questions.solution: in

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Correct: It is high time we leave for the station.
options: It is high time we leave for the station.
It is high time we have left for the station.
It is high time we were leaving for the station.
It is high time we left for the station.
questions.solution: It is high time we left for the station.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: She speaks ____ softly that no one can hear her.
questions.solution: so

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Correct: She is one of the most talented singer in the industry.
options: She is one of the most talented singer in the industry.
She is one of the talented singer in the industry.
She is one of the most talented singers in the industry.
She is the most talented singer in the industry.
questions.solution: She is one of the most talented singers in the industry.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: The manager was angry ____ the delay.
questions.solution: about

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Correct: The committee have submitted its report.
options: The committee have submitted its report.
The committee has submitted their report.
The committee has submitted its report.
The committee have submitted their report.
questions.solution: The committee has submitted its report.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: He was prevented ____ attending the meeting.
questions.solution: from

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Correct: He is more cleverer than his brother.
options: He is more clever than his brother.
He is clever than his brother.
He is more cleverer than his brother.
He is cleverer than his brother.
questions.solution: He is cleverer than his brother.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: The book is superior ____ the other one.
questions.solution: to

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Correct: He asked that whether I could help him.
options: He asked that whether I could help him.
He asked if whether I could help him.
He asked that I could help him.
He asked whether I could help him.
questions.solution: He asked whether I could help him.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: She is capable ____ doing the job independently.
questions.solution: of

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Correct: She not only lost her wallet but also her phone.
options: She not only lost her wallet but also lost.
She lost not only her wallet but also.
She not only lost her wallet but also her phone.
She lost not only her wallet but also her phone.
questions.solution: She lost not only her wallet but also her phone.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: The patient died ____ a heart attack.
questions.solution: of

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Correct: He is better than any student in the class.
options: He is best than any student in the class.
He is better than any other student in the class.
He is the best than any student in the class.
He is better than any student in the class.
questions.solution: He is better than any other student in the class.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: ____ you work hard, you will not succeed.
questions.solution: Unless

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Correct: He told the news to his brother and I.
options: He told the news to I and my brother.
He told the news to his brother and me.
He told the news to his brother and I.
He told the news to my brother and myself.
questions.solution: He told the news to his brother and me.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Antonym of LETHARGIC:
questions.solution: Energetic

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Find the error: I have (A) / been working (B) / in this organisation (C) / since ten years. (D)
questions.solution: D - should be 'for ten years'

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Meaning of 'To be a sitting duck':
questions.solution: To be an easy target

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Correct: The informations provided is accurate.
options: The information provided has been accurate.
questions.solution: The information provided is accurate.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Synonym of JEOPARDIZE:
questions.solution: Endanger

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Correct: He asked me to convey him my regards.
options: He asked that I convey him my regards.
He asked me to convey to him my regards.
He asked me to convey my regards to him.
He asked me to convey him my regards.
questions.solution: He asked me to convey to him my regards.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Synonym of HARMONY:
questions.solution: Concord

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Antonym of LACONIC:
questions.solution: Verbose

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Normalization 3NF requires:
options: 2NF + no transitive dependency (non-key depends on another non-key)
questions.solution: 3NF: no transitive dependencies. X → Y and Y → Z but Y not key → move Z to new table.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The Boolean expression A(A + B) simplifies to:
questions.solution: A(A + B) = A + AB = A (absorption law).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Peephole optimization operates on:
options: A small window of instructions (adjacent few)
questions.solution: Peephole optimization: local improvements like redundant load/store removal.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The vertex cover problem asks:
options: Find the smallest set of vertices that covers all edges
questions.solution: Vertex cover: minimum set of vertices touching all edges. NP-complete.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Two-phase locking (2PL) ensures:
options: Conflict serializability (growing phase then shrinking phase)
questions.solution: 2PL: acquire all locks (growing), then release (shrinking).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A synchronous counter:
questions.solution: Synchronous counter: common clock, parallel operation (faster, more complex wiring).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A lexeme is:
options: A sequence of characters that forms a token
questions.solution: Lexeme = actual string matched. Token = lexeme + token type.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: T(n) = 3T(n/2) + n² solves to:
questions.solution: Master theorem: a=3, b=2, f(n)=n². logₐb = log₂3 ≈ 1.58 < 2. Case 3: O(n²).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: 3NF is violated when:
options: A table has no foreign key
A non-key attribute depends transitively on the primary key
questions.solution: 3NF = no transitive dependency (non-key → non-key).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The multiplexer (MUX) selects:
options: One of many inputs based on select lines
questions.solution: MUX: n select lines → 2^n inputs, 1 output.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Global common subexpression elimination works:
options: Across basic blocks within a function
questions.solution: Global CSE: eliminates expressions computed multiple times in different blocks.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Modular exponentiation (a^b mod m) can be computed in:
options: O(log b) using exponentiation by squaring
questions.solution: Exponentiation by squaring: decompose b in binary → O(log b) multiplications.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Two-phase locking (2PL) protocol:
options: Locks and releases at any time
Expanding phase (acquire locks) → Shrinking phase (release locks, no new acquires)
questions.solution: 2PL: guarantees serializability. Strict 2PL: all exclusive locks held until commit (prevents cascading aborts).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Race-around condition occurs in:
options: JK flip-flop (when J=K=1 and clock pulse too long)
questions.solution: Race-around: JK with J=K=1 oscillates if clock pulse > propagation delay. Solved by master-slave.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Instruction scheduling:
options: Reorders instructions to avoid pipeline stalls and improve ILP
questions.solution: Instruction scheduling: reorder independent instructions to separate dependent ones (hide latency).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The worst-case time complexity of mergesort for n=500 is:
questions.solution: Mergesort always O(n log n). For n=500: O(500 log 500).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A tuple in relational model is:
questions.solution: Tuple = row/record. Attribute = column/field. Relation = table.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The Boolean expression (A + B)(A + C) simplifies to:
questions.solution: (A + B)(A + C) = A + BC (by Boolean algebra).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A DAG for basic block optimization:
options: Represents common sub-expressions as shared nodes
questions.solution: DAG = Directed Acyclic Graph. Shares common subexpressions (CSE elimination).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The master theorem solves recurrences of the form:
questions.solution: Master theorem: T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n) for divide-and-conquer recurrences.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A deadlock in databases occurs when:
options: Two or more transactions hold locks and wait for each other's locks
questions.solution: Deadlock detection: wait-for graph (cycle = deadlock). Victim selected and rolled back.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: For the AND gate shown with inputs A = 1 and B = 0, the output is:

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 220 110" width="220" height="110"><line x1="10" y1="35" x2="70" y2="35" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="10" y1="75" x2="70" y2="75" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="2"/><text x="6" y="30" font-size="11" fill="#2563eb">A=1</text><text x="6" y="92" font-size="11" fill="#2563eb">B=0</text><path d="M70 20 L110 20 Q150 55 110 90 L70 90 Z" fill="#f1f5f9" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="95" y="60" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle" fill="#475569">AND</text><line x1="150" y1="55" x2="200" y2="55" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="2"/><text x="180" y="48" font-size="11" fill="#dc2626">?</text></svg>
figure.caption: AND gate

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Lex is a:
questions.solution: Lex = tokenizer generator (regex patterns → action code).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A polynomial-time reduction from A to B proves:
options: If A is in NP, B is in P
If A is NP-hard, B is NP
If B is in P, then A is also in P
questions.solution: Reduction A ≤_p B: A is no harder than B (B is at least as hard as A).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A view in SQL is:
options: A virtual table based on the result of a stored query
questions.solution: View: saved query (not data). Updatable views have restrictions. Materialized view = physical copy.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: For the XOR gate shown with inputs A = 0 and B = 1, the output is:

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 220 110" width="220" height="110"><line x1="10" y1="35" x2="70" y2="35" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="10" y1="75" x2="70" y2="75" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="2"/><text x="6" y="30" font-size="11" fill="#2563eb">A=0</text><text x="6" y="92" font-size="11" fill="#2563eb">B=1</text><path d="M70 20 L110 20 Q150 55 110 90 L70 90 Z" fill="#f1f5f9" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="95" y="60" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle" fill="#475569">XOR</text><line x1="150" y1="55" x2="200" y2="55" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="2"/><text x="180" y="48" font-size="11" fill="#dc2626">?</text></svg>
figure.caption: XOR gate

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The activation record (stack frame) stores:
options: Local variables, return address, parameters, and saved registers
questions.solution: Activation record created on call stack for each function invocation.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The worst-case time complexity of mergesort for n=100 is:
questions.solution: Mergesort always O(n log n). For n=100: O(100 log 100).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: CAP theorem states:
options: Consistency, Availability, Partition tolerance: at most 2 of 3
questions.solution: CAP theorem (Brewer): in a distributed system, choose CP, AP, or CA (but CA not possible in practice).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The propagation delay of a logic gate is:
options: The time from input change to output change
questions.solution: Propagation delay: t_P = (t_PHL + t_PLH)/2 typically.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Activation record (stack frame) contains:
options: Return address, parameters, local variables, saved registers
questions.solution: Activation record: pushed on call stack when function invoked. Popped on return.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The number of nodes in a complete binary tree of height h=4 (root at 0) is at most:
questions.solution: Max nodes in complete binary tree of height h = 2^(h+1)-1 = 31.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The SQL DISTINCT keyword:
options: Removes duplicate rows from the result
questions.solution: SELECT DISTINCT = unique rows only.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The don't-care condition in K-map:
options: Can be used as 0 or 1 to simplify the expression
questions.solution: Don't-care (X): can be assigned 0 or 1 to form larger groups.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Handle in LR parsing is:
options: The substring matching the RHS of a production being reduced
questions.solution: Handle = RHS of a production that can be reduced at current step.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A greedy algorithm always makes:
options: The locally optimal choice at each step
questions.solution: Greedy: local optimum at each step (may not yield global optimum for some problems).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A candidate key is:
options: A minimal superkey (no proper subset is a superkey)
A primary key chosen by DBA
questions.solution: Candidate key: minimal set of attributes that uniquely identify a tuple.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: For the AND gate shown with inputs A = 0 and B = 1, the output is:

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 220 110" width="220" height="110"><line x1="10" y1="35" x2="70" y2="35" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="10" y1="75" x2="70" y2="75" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="2"/><text x="6" y="30" font-size="11" fill="#2563eb">A=0</text><text x="6" y="92" font-size="11" fill="#2563eb">B=1</text><path d="M70 20 L110 20 Q150 55 110 90 L70 90 Z" fill="#f1f5f9" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="95" y="60" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle" fill="#475569">AND</text><line x1="150" y1="55" x2="200" y2="55" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="2"/><text x="180" y="48" font-size="11" fill="#dc2626">?</text></svg>
figure.caption: AND gate

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The LR(1) item includes:
questions.solution: LR(1): [A → α·β, a] where a = lookahead (reduces only when next input = a).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The longest common subsequence (LCS) problem uses:
questions.solution: LCS = DP with 2D table of size (m+1)×(n+1).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A relation in BCNF is also in 3NF, but the converse may fail when:
options: A non-key attribute determines part of a candidate key
questions.solution: BCNF is stricter: every determinant must be a superkey; 3NF allows certain prime-attribute dependencies.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The number of full adders needed for a 4-bit binary adder is:
questions.solution: n-bit adder needs n full adders (one per bit position).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The phases of a compiler include:
options: Lexical, syntax, semantic analysis, intermediate code, optimization, code generation
questions.solution: Compiler phases: frontend (analysis) + backend (synthesis).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The worst-case time complexity of mergesort for n=32 is:
questions.solution: Mergesort always O(n log n). For n=32: O(32 log 32).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Referential integrity constraint:
options: Ensures that a foreign key value has a matching primary key in the referenced table
questions.solution: Referential integrity: FK value must exist in parent table or be NULL (if nullable).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The sum of minterms expression is also called:
questions.solution: SOP: OR of AND terms (minterms). POS: AND of OR terms (maxterms).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: An LL(1) grammar must be:
questions.solution: LL(1): no left recursion, unambiguous, FIRST/FOLLOW sets don't conflict.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Fractional knapsack uses:
questions.solution: Fractional knapsack = greedy by value/weight ratio (optimal).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: REPEATABLE READ prevents:
questions.solution: REPEATABLE READ: same read returns same result (but phantom inserts may appear).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Adjacent cells in a K-map differ by:
questions.solution: K-map: cells arranged in Gray code order (adjacent differ by 1 variable).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Chaitin's algorithm for register allocation:
options: Simplifies graph by removing nodes with degree < k (can be colored easily)
Colors all nodes with greedy algorithm
questions.solution: If simplification leaves nodes (degree ≥ k): spill (move to memory). Optimistic coloring (Briggs) may avoid spill.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The solution of T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n is:
questions.solution: Master theorem case 2: a=2, b=2, f(n)=n = n^(log₂(2)) → T(n) = O(n log n).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A trigger in SQL:
options: Automatically executes in response to a database event (INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE)
questions.solution: Trigger: BEFORE/AFTER, row-level/statement-level. Used for auditing, validation, cascading actions.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A ripple counter has the disadvantage of:
questions.solution: Ripple counter: each FF clocked by previous output → cumulative delay.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Canonical LR(1) has:
options: Many more states than LR(0) or LALR
questions.solution: Canonical LR(1): most powerful LR variant but may have 1000s of states (vs ~100 for LALR).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The number of comparisons in selection sort for n=256 is:
questions.solution: Selection sort: 256*(256-1)/2 = 32640 comparisons (always).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Timestamp ordering (TO) protocol:
options: Assigns timestamps to transactions; conflicts resolved by aborting the older transaction
questions.solution: TO: if T1(write) conflicts with T2(read/write) and T1 is older → T2 aborts (Thomas Write Rule exceptions).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The output of a NAND gate is 0 when:
questions.solution: NAND: output = (A·B)'. Only 0 when A=1 and B=1.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The 'test-and-set' instruction provides:
questions.solution: T&S = atomic hardware instruction for implementing locks.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: BCNF is stronger than 3NF:
options: Every determinant must be a candidate key
questions.solution: BCNF: every FD X → Y must have X as superkey. Eliminates all redundancy from FDs.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Operations on an empty stack: push 7, push 8, push 14, pop, push 21. The element now on top is:
questions.solution: After push 14 then pop (removes 14), pushing 21 leaves 21 on top (LIFO).

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 220 130" width="220" height="130"><rect x="80" y="20" width="60" height="24" fill="#fee2e2" stroke="#dc2626"/><text x="110" y="37" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">21</text><rect x="80" y="44" width="60" height="24" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="110" y="61" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">8</text><rect x="80" y="68" width="60" height="24" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="110" y="85" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">7</text><line x1="78" y1="20" x2="78" y2="92" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="142" y1="20" x2="142" y2="92" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="78" y1="92" x2="142" y2="92" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="2"/><text x="150" y="30" font-size="10" fill="#dc2626">← top</text></svg>
figure.caption: Stack state

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The hexadecimal representation of decimal 511 is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Fast retransmit is triggered by:
questions.solution: Fast retransmit: 3 dup ACKs = packet lost, retransmit without waiting for timeout.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The dirty bit (modified bit) indicates:
options: The page has been written to (needs to be written back to disk)
questions.solution: Dirty bit: if 0, page can be evicted without writing to swap (clean copy on disk).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The LRU page replacement policy replaces:
questions.solution: LRU = replace page with oldest access time.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The Huffman coding algorithm uses:
questions.solution: Huffman: greedy (merge two smallest frequencies) using min-heap.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The TCP timestamp option:
options: Enables RTT measurement and PAWS (Protection Against Wrapped Sequences)
questions.solution: Timestamp: sender puts current time, receiver echoes back. PAWS prevents old duplicate segments.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Superscalar processors can:
options: Execute multiple instructions per clock cycle
questions.solution: Superscalar: multiple execution units (ILP - instruction-level parallelism).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The ACID properties of a transaction stand for:
questions.solution: ACID guarantees reliable transaction processing.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A spinlock is:
options: A lock where the thread busy-waits (spins) in a loop
questions.solution: Spinlock = busy-wait (good for short critical sections, no context switch overhead).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The working set model defines:
options: The set of pages a process is currently using
questions.solution: Working set = Δ most recent page references. If WS > available RAM → thrashing.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The shortest remaining time first (SRTF) is:
questions.solution: SRTF = preemptive SJF (switch when new process has shorter remaining time).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Belady's anomaly occurs with:
questions.solution: Belady: in FIFO, increasing frames can sometimes increase page faults (not monotonic).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Paging is a memory-management scheme that maps:
questions.solution: Paging divides memory into fixed-size pages/frames and uses a page table to map them.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The mutex lock provides:
options: Mutual exclusion (only one thread can acquire the lock)
questions.solution: Mutex = binary semaphore (only one owner).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Demand paging brings a page into memory:
options: Only when it is first referenced (causing a page fault)
At program load time, all at once
questions.solution: Pages are loaded lazily on first access; the page fault triggers the load.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The graph isomorphism problem:
options: Is in NP but not known to be NP-complete
questions.solution: Graph isomorphism: in NP, not known NP-complete. Babai (2015): quasipolynomial time.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The 'aging' technique prevents:
options: Starvation by gradually increasing priority of waiting processes
questions.solution: Aging: priority of waiting process increases over time → eventually chosen.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Operations on an empty stack: push 7, push 9, push 12, pop, push 25. The element now on top is:
questions.solution: After push 12 then pop (removes 12), pushing 25 leaves 25 on top (LIFO).

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 220 130" width="220" height="130"><rect x="80" y="20" width="60" height="24" fill="#fee2e2" stroke="#dc2626"/><text x="110" y="37" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">25</text><rect x="80" y="44" width="60" height="24" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="110" y="61" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">9</text><rect x="80" y="68" width="60" height="24" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="110" y="85" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">7</text><line x1="78" y1="20" x2="78" y2="92" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="142" y1="20" x2="142" y2="92" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="78" y1="92" x2="142" y2="92" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="2"/><text x="150" y="30" font-size="10" fill="#dc2626">← top</text></svg>
figure.caption: Stack state

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Operations on an empty stack: push 5, push 9, push 12, pop, push 25. The element now on top is:
questions.solution: After push 12 then pop (removes 12), pushing 25 leaves 25 on top (LIFO).

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 220 130" width="220" height="130"><rect x="80" y="20" width="60" height="24" fill="#fee2e2" stroke="#dc2626"/><text x="110" y="37" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">25</text><rect x="80" y="44" width="60" height="24" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="110" y="61" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">9</text><rect x="80" y="68" width="60" height="24" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="110" y="85" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">5</text><line x1="78" y1="20" x2="78" y2="92" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="142" y1="20" x2="142" y2="92" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="78" y1="92" x2="142" y2="92" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="2"/><text x="150" y="30" font-size="10" fill="#dc2626">← top</text></svg>
figure.caption: Stack state

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Demand paging loads pages:
options: Only when a page fault occurs
questions.solution: Demand paging = lazy loading; pages brought in on page fault.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A hash table with 50 slots holds 45 keys. Its load factor is:
questions.solution: Load factor α = n/m = 45/50 = 0.9.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: For the binary search tree shown (root 50), the in-order traversal sequence is:
questions.solution: In-order (left–root–right) of a BST gives ascending order: 20, 25, 33, 50, 65.

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 260 150" width="260" height="150"><line x1="130" y1="30" x2="80" y2="75" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="130" y1="30" x2="190" y2="75" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="80" y1="75" x2="50" y2="120" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="80" y1="75" x2="110" y2="120" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5"/><circle cx="130" cy="30" r="16" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="130" y="34" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">50</text><circle cx="80" cy="75" r="16" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="80" y="79" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">25</text><circle cx="190" cy="75" r="16" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="190" y="79" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">65</text><circle cx="50" cy="120" r="16" fill="#dcfce7" stroke="#16a34a"/><text x="50" y="124" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">20</text><circle cx="110" cy="120" r="16" fill="#dcfce7" stroke="#16a34a"/><text x="110" y="124" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">33</text></svg>
figure.caption: Binary search tree

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The maximum number of comparisons made by bubble sort on 5 elements is:
questions.solution: Bubble sort makes n(n−1)/2 = 5×4/2 = 10 comparisons.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A perfect binary tree of height 5 (root at height 1) has how many nodes?

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The XOR gate outputs 1 when:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The scheduler selects:
options: Which process/thread runs next on the CPU
questions.solution: Scheduler: makes scheduling decisions (short-term scheduler = dispatcher).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The state diagram of a Mealy machine has:
questions.solution: Mealy: output = f(input, current state). Moore: output = f(current state only).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: In a binary tree, the maximum number of nodes at level k (0-indexed) is:
questions.solution: Level 0: 1 = 2°, Level 1: 2 = 2¹, Level k: 2^k.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Church numerals represent:
questions.solution: Church: 0 = λf.λx.x, 1 = λf.λx.f x, 2 = λf.λx.f(f x). Successor = λn.λf.λx.f(n f x).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The Banker's algorithm prevents:
options: Deadlock by checking if allocation leaves a safe state
questions.solution: Banker (Dijkstra): process declares max need. Grant only if safe state remains.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A primary key:
options: Uniquely identifies each row in a table
questions.solution: Primary key = unique, NOT NULL, identifies rows.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Cohesion measures:
options: How closely the elements within a module are related
How modules depend on each other
The number of functions in a module
questions.solution: Cohesion (best→worst): functional, sequential, communicational, procedural, temporal, logical, coincidental.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A counting semaphore can be initialized to:
questions.solution: Counting sem: init to number of available resources.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: CSMA/CD (Ethernet) handles collisions by:
options: Detecting collision, sending jam signal, backing off (exponential backoff)
questions.solution: CSMA/CD: Carrier Sense Multiple Access / Collision Detection. Binary exponential backoff after collision.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A counter that counts 0-7 is a:
questions.solution: 3 bits = 8 states (0 to 7). Mod-8 counter.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The number of rows in the truth table for n inputs is:
questions.solution: Truth table has 2^n rows (all possible input combinations).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A foreign key:
options: References the primary key of another table
questions.solution: Foreign key = column(s) referencing PK of another table (referential integrity).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: How many address lines are needed to address 1K memory locations?
questions.solution: 1K = 1024 = 2^10, so 10 address lines are required.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The instruction cycle consists of:
questions.solution: Instruction cycle: fetch → decode → execute → (write back).
